metadata.summary: Map end-to-end flow from customer request to value using Flow Metrics and DORA; identify bottleneck via Theory of Constraints; run targeted experiments to elevate the constraint.

content.title: Value Stream Management
content.summary: Map the end-to-end flow from customer request to customer value, measure it with Flow Metrics (Lead Time, Cycle Time, Throughput, WIP, %C/A) and DORA (Deployment Frequency, Change Lead Time, Change Failure Rate, MTTR), identify the constraint per Theory of Constraints, and run targeted experiments to elevate that constraint. Instrument with telemetry first — a VSM workshop without data decays into a poster within months.

why.p: AI productivity gains and DevOps automation frequently fail to improve customer-visible delivery time because the bottleneck lies outside the software team (product spec, design review, compliance gate, support queue). Flow Metrics expose end-to-end flow; DORA measures only DevOps efficiency. Used together they locate where value actually stalls, preventing teams from optimizing the wrong stage.

list.item: Engineering org has shipped DevOps automation but customer-visible lead time has not improved
Cross-functional bottleneck suspected across product → design → eng → release → support
Quarterly OKR cycle wants to move from output metrics to flow metrics
DORA metrics are already in place but not improving — need upstream view via Flow Metrics
Org adopting SAFe, FAST Agile, or the Project-to-Product model
Single-team, single-product startup pre-PMF — premature optimization
Org without telemetry baseline (no commit timestamps, no deploy log) — instrument first
Pure cost-cutting context — VSM exposes inefficiencies but is not a layoff lever
Teams with no shared ownership across the stream — VSM names the bottleneck but cannot move it

section.name: value-stream-mapping-fundamentals

rule.statement: Map from "user filed request" to "user received value" — not from "ticket created" to "merged"; customer-visible time is the only honest measurement boundary.
rule.rationale: Internal pipeline metrics (commit-to-deploy) can look elite while the customer waits weeks for spec, design, and compliance stages that precede the first commit. Only customer-anchored boundaries reveal actual value flow.

section.p: VSM originated from lean manufacturing; Mik Kersten (Project to Product, 2018) adapted it to software with the Flow Framework. It identifies bottlenecks outside the software team — the stages that DevOps automation cannot reach.

section.name: flow-metrics
section.p: Flow Metrics from Kersten / Flow Framework.

list.item: Lead Time: time from accepting work to customer handoff — end-to-end flow. The primary metric.
Cycle Time: time within a single process stage — process efficiency. Used to locate the constraint.
Throughput: items completed per time period — delivery rate.
WIP: work items in progress simultaneously — per Little's Law, WIP = Throughput × Cycle Time.
%C/A (Complete and Accurate): percentage received by downstream stage without rework needed — quality of handoffs.

rule.statement: Split flow data by work type (feature, defect, risk, debt) — single-number flow metrics hide trade-offs.
rule.rationale: A team shipping 100% defect work has high throughput but zero feature delivery; aggregating disguises this. Mik Kersten's four-type split is the minimum granularity.

section.name: dora-four-tuple
section.p: DORA metrics from Forsgren, Humble, Kim (2018).

list.item: Deployment Frequency: how often code deploys to production. Elite = multiple per day.
Change Lead Time: commit to production time. Elite = under 1 hour.
Change Failure Rate: percentage of deployments causing a failure. Elite = under 5%.
Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR): time to restore service after failure. Elite = under 1 hour.

rule.statement: Never optimize one DORA metric in isolation — always pair DF with CFR; high DF + high CFR is a negative trade-off.
rule.rationale: Deployment frequency can be gamed by deploying trivial changes; the constraint is value delivery per deploy, not deploys per day in isolation.

rule.statement: Do not publish "Elite DORA" status without trend context — the label is meaningless and will be gamed by teams optimizing for the scorecard.
rule.rationale: DORA levels were designed as benchmarks for improvement, not badges. Publishing static levels without trend data invites Goodhart's Law to destroy the signal.

list.item: 1. Instrument: connect Jira/Linear, GitHub, deploy logs, PagerDuty, Zendesk via ID links — commit timestamps are the foundation.
2. Map: trace one work item end-to-end; identify the stages; measure Lead Time and Cycle Time per stage.
3. Measure DORA: daily collection on git + deploy logs.
4. Identify constraint: apply Little's Law; highest WIP × Cycle Time stage is the system bottleneck (Theory of Constraints).
5. Experiment: run one targeted experiment on the constraint; measure its effect on Lead Time.

section.name: agentic-vsm-pipeline

rule.statement: A flow-instrumenter subagent maps the actual value stream by tracing IDs across tools (issue → branch → PR → deploy → incident → support ticket), normalizes timestamps into stream stages, and emits per-stage metrics.
rule.rationale: Manual cross-tool ID linking is error-prone and stale within a week; automated tracing from authoritative event logs is the only way to maintain a live VSM.

section.p: A DORA collector runs daily on git + deploy logs. A bottleneck-analyzer applies Theory of Constraints weekly, flags the constraint stage, and proposes 1-3 experiments. Humans choose which experiment to run; agents track its effect.
section.reference: Schema for a single traced work item across VSM stages
Bash script to compute last-30-day DORA from git + deploy log

list.item: flow-instrumenter — wires Jira/Linear, GitHub, deploy logs, Sentry; traces item IDs cross-tool; produces stream JSON per work item.
dora-collector — daily DORA: deploy frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, MTTR from git + PagerDuty.
bottleneck-analyzer — applies Little's Law; identifies constraint by highest (cycle time × WIP); requires at least 50 items per work type before declaring a bottleneck.
experiment-tracker — A/B's a process change (e.g., trunk-based vs. feature-branch) on flow metrics; tracks before/after lead time distribution.

example.note: Flow-instrumenter prompt — skips items with missing stages rather than hallucinating timestamps
example.code: You are a flow-instrumenter. Trace work item {issue_id} across tools using mapping {tool_id_links.yaml}. Emit JSON {item_id, stages: [{name, enter_ts, exit_ts, blocked_h, type}], outcome: shipped|cancelled, work_type: feature| defect|risk|debt}. Skip items with > 1 missing stage — do not interpolate. All timestamps in UTC ISO 8601.

example.note: Bottleneck-analyzer prompt — requires minimum sample before declaring constraint
example.code: You are a bottleneck-analyzer. Given last 90 days of flow data {flow.json}, return: (1) constraint stage by Little's Law (highest cycle time x WIP), (2) flow distribution across work types, (3) top 3 experiments to elevate the constraint, ranked by expected throughput lift / cost. Require >= 50 items per work type before declaring a bottleneck. Flag insufficient data explicitly rather than guessing.

antipattern.description: Agent hallucinates ID links across tools using loose regex matching.
antipattern.reason: Require explicit referenced-by chains in source systems; reject inferred links that lack audit trail.

antipattern.description: Timezone bugs in lead-time calculation — agent uses local time, losing UTC anchor.
antipattern.reason: Force UTC; never local. Reconcile agent output against a known-good calculation.

antipattern.description: DORA "elite" is published as a scorecard without trend context.
antipattern.reason: DORA levels are meaningless without trend. Publish as "up from X% last quarter" not "we are elite".

antipattern.description: Webhook-driven instrumentation drops events under load.
antipattern.reason: Reconcile with periodic full pulls; webhooks are real-time hints, not the authoritative feed.

antipattern.description: AI commits flood the repo with micro-PRs that distort throughput.
antipattern.reason: Tag bot PRs and report human/bot split; do not mix them into DORA.

antipattern.description: "Reliability" (5th DORA metric) is org-defined; agent picks the SLO target.
antipattern.reason: Humans define reliability SLO; agents implement measurement only.

antipattern.description: Constraint identification flips month-to-month with low data volume.
antipattern.reason: Require ≥ 50 items per type before declaring a bottleneck; flag insufficient data explicitly.

list.item: four-keys (Google) — DORA pipeline reference implementation (https://github.com/dora-team/fourkeys)
gh api — GitHub deployments + PR timestamps for DORA collection
jq — stream join JSON across tools; essential for cross-tool ID linking (https://jqlang.github.io/jq/)
git log --pretty — cycle time inputs from commit timestamps (core git)
pagerduty-cli — MTTR data from incident logs (https://github.com/martindstone/pagerduty-cli)
glab api — GitLab deployment events, MR timestamps
LinearB — engineering metrics, GitHub-native, REST API; good for mid-size orgs (SaaS).
Plandek — Flow + DORA dashboards, REST API; supports multiple source integrations (SaaS).
Jellyfish — engineering benchmarking, REST API; strong on team-level analysis (SaaS).
Faros AI — DORA + Flow with open data layer; can self-host (SaaS/OSS).
Apache DevLake — self-host DORA + Flow with plugin architecture for all major tools (OSS).
Tasktop / Planview Viz — enterprise VSM platform; requires license; REST API partial (SaaS).
Code Climate Velocity — cycle time, PR throughput; GitHub-centric (SaaS).
Allstacks — forecasting from flow data; REST API (SaaS).

rule.statement: Visualize flow with cumulative flow diagram (CFD), not bar charts — CFD reveals stage starvation and WIP accumulation that bar charts hide.
rule.rationale: Bar charts show throughput at a point in time; CFDs show the rate of item entry and exit per stage, making bottleneck dynamics visible as widening bands.

rule.statement: Use Little's Law (WIP = Throughput × Cycle Time) as a sanity check on agent-collected data — large deviations indicate instrumentation errors.
rule.rationale: Little's Law holds for any stable system; if agent-collected WIP, throughput, and cycle time do not satisfy it, there is a data collection bug, not an anomaly.

rule.statement: When AI tooling lifts throughput, watch Change Failure Rate and rework rate — productivity that creates incidents is disguised debt.
rule.rationale: The AI productivity paradox: AI-generated code that deploys fast but fails in production increases MTTR and CFR, erasing the DF gain. Track the full four-tuple together.

rule.statement: Split flow data by work type (Mik Kersten's four: feature, defect, risk, debt) — single-number flow lies.
rule.rationale: A team shipping 100% defect work has high throughput but zero feature velocity; aggregating hides the truth.

rule.statement: Never optimize one metric in isolation; always pair (e.g., DF up + CFR up = bad trade).
rule.rationale: DORA metrics interact; optimizing one in isolation breaks others.

rule.statement: Anchor VSM in customer-visible time: from "user filed request" to "user received value", not "ticket created" to "merged".
rule.rationale: Internal metrics can look elite while the customer waits in queue; customer-visible time is the only honest measure.

rule.statement: Run quarterly VSM workshop; refresh map; do not assume the stream stays static.
rule.rationale: VSM is a snapshot; the value stream changes as teams reorganize and tooling evolves.

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: Flow Metrics aggregate work types (feature/defect/risk/debt) and hide trade-offs if not split by type. DORA is a four-tuple proxy, not the actual customer outcome — Elite DORA + falling NPS happens. VSM workshops produce posters, not telemetry — without instrumentation, the map decays in months. Cross-team flow data lives in 5+ tools (Jira, GitHub, PagerDuty, Salesforce, Zendesk) with mismatched IDs. "%C/A" (complete and accurate) is hard to measure without explicit handoff QA gates. AI-generated PRs can inflate throughput and Deployment Frequency without raising Earned Value.

template.purpose: Bash script: compute last-30-day DORA from git log + deploy.log
Schema for a single traced work item across VSM stages
